case_name: Allen v. Sarah Bush Lincoln Health Center

summary.brief: Allen v. Sarah Bush Lincoln Health Center. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: On September 21, 2012, plaintiff, Mark Allen, felt a 'pop' in his neck and began to experience neck pain.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Allen was given painkillers and discharged. The following day, September 22, 2012, Allen again went to defendant’s emergency room and, again, was given painkillers and discharged.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On September 26, 2012, Allen’s condition worsened. He went to defendant’s emergency room where he was seen by Dr. Derek Stout, who ordered a computerized tomography (CT) scan for Allen.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Allen collapsed and was taken to defendant’s emergency room via ambulance before being transferred to Carle Hospital early the following morning.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: At Carle, a neurologist examined Allen and ordered a magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scan, which revealed that Allen had a spinal epidural abscess.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Allen had spinal surgery to drain the abscess but still suffered a spinal cord injury as a result of the abscess.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The jury returned a $14 million verdict in favor of Allen.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Raja testified that Allen presented to Stout with the classic triad of symptoms for a spinal epidural abscess: (1) fever, (2) pain radiating down the back, and (3) neurological changes such as tingling and hypersensitive skin on the shoulders.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Ali Raja

evidence_index.text: Raja opined that Stout should have put it at the top of his differential diagnosis, not the bottom; further, Stout should have immediately ordered an MRI.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Ali Raja

evidence_index.text: Kopin opined that the standard of care for any physician, regardless of specialty, required an MRI of Allen’s cervical spine.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Jeff Kopin

evidence_index.text: Bernstein opined that if Allen had been operated on prior to September 28, it was more likely than not true that Allen would have spent five to seven days in the hospital, would have had a full neurological recovery, and would have returned to normal activities.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Avi Bernstein

evidence_index.text: Melissa Taylor testified that she was Allen’s ex-wife and the mother of his children.
evidence_index.speaker: Melissa Taylor

evidence_index.text: Taylor testified that Allen began feeling severe pain on September 20, 2012.
evidence_index.speaker: Melissa Taylor

evidence_index.text: Allen testified that on September 21, 2012, he was moving boxes at his mother’s house when he felt pain and numbness in his neck.
evidence_index.speaker: Mark Allen

evidence_index.text: Allen’s blood was tested at around 12:35 p.m. and showed he had an abnormally high white blood cell count.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court granted in part defendant’s motion for partial summary judgment, stating that it found no genuine issue of material fact regarding actual agency because Dale was not an actual agent of the hospital.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial court denied Allen’s motion for leave to file the second amended complaint because, in part, (1) Allen’s original complaint was based solely on Stout’s negligence and never alleged Dale made a mistake.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.chief_complaint: Severe neck pain and tingling sensation
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: On September 21, 2012, patient felt a 'pop' in his neck while moving boxes, followed by severe neck pain. Presented to the emergency department with neck pain and was discharged with painkillers. Returned on September 22 with persistent pain and fever, again discharged with painkillers. On September 26, presented with worsening symptoms including severe neck pain, tingling in hands, and burning sensation in shoulders.

vital_signs.temperature: Intermittent fever reported, specific values not documented

focused_exam.neurological: Tingling in hands, burning sensation in shoulders

requestables.name: Complete Blood Count
requestables.description: Blood test to check for infection

reveal.decision_impact: Should prompt further investigation for source of infection

requestables.name: CT Scan of Neck
requestables.description: Imaging to check for abscess or other abnormalities

reveal.detailed_findings: CT scan read as normal, no abscess detected
reveal.clinical_significance: Did not rule out spinal epidural abscess
reveal.decision_impact: Should have prompted MRI for definitive diagnosis

requestables.name: MRI of Cervical Spine
requestables.description: Definitive imaging for spinal epidural abscess

reveal.detailed_findings: Not performed initially; later MRI revealed spinal epidural abscess
reveal.clinical_significance: Confirmed diagnosis of spinal epidural abscess
reveal.decision_impact: Would have led to earlier surgical intervention

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with neck pain

key_events.event: Decision to order CT instead of MRI

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with severe neck pain and neurological symptoms
decision_points.prompt: What imaging investigation should be ordered?

options.clinical_reasoning: MRI is the definitive test for spinal epidural abscess
CT scan is not sufficient to rule out spinal epidural abscess

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Believed CT scan was sufficient to rule out abscess

expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert testimony established MRI as standard for suspected spinal epidural abscess

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: CT scan does not adequately rule out spinal epidural abscess
explanation.what_should_have_happened: MRI should have been performed to confirm diagnosis
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony

patient_outcome.description: Patient suffered spinal cord injury due to delayed diagnosis

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to order MRI
Causation established - earlier diagnosis would have prevented spinal cord injury

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: MRI would have shown spinal epidural abscess

factual_timeline.event: Patient felt a 'pop' in neck and presented to ED
Patient presented with worsening symptoms, CT scan performed
Patient reports neck pain and 'pop' sensation
Patient's condition worsens, goes to emergency room
Patient felt a 'pop' in neck and experienced pain

court_findings.legal_significance: Failure to order MRI was a breach
